heroOverview: Hypopituitarism is the deficiency of one or more hormones produced or regulated by the pituitary gland, a pea-sized master endocrine gland at the base of the brain that governs the thyroid, adrenal glands, gonads, growth, and water balance. The anterior pituitary secretes growth hormone, ACTH, TSH, LH, FSH, and prolactin; the posterior pituitary releases ADH and oxytocin. Any lesion, inflammation, or injury that damages the pituitary or its hypothalamic connections can impair these axes. The condition affects approximately 45 per 100,000 people worldwide, causes significant morbidity through fatigue, adrenal crisis, infertility, and metabolic disruption, and requires lifelong hormone replacement therapy tailored to each deficient axis. With complete and consistent replacement, many patients lead fully normal lives.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 45 per 100,000 in population-based studies; incidence roughly 4 per 100,000 per year
keyStats.demographics: Can affect all ages and both sexes; pituitary adenoma is the most common cause in adults, craniopharyngioma and trauma in younger patients
keyStats.avgAge: Diagnosis peaks in adults aged 30–60, though congenital and childhood forms occur

definition: Hypopituitarism (ICD-10: E23.0) describes the partial or complete loss of anterior and/or posterior pituitary hormone secretion, classified by the number of axes involved (monotropic — a single hormone; plurihormonal — multiple hormones; panhypopituitarism — all anterior axes). Because the pituitary sits in the sella turcica and its stalk passes through the diaphragma sellae, any space-occupying lesion, infiltrative process, or vascular event in this region can compress or destroy functioning pituitary cells. The most clinically urgent deficiency is ACTH insufficiency, which causes secondary adrenal insufficiency and risks potentially fatal adrenal crisis if not replaced during illness or surgery. TSH deficiency produces secondary hypothyroidism indistinguishable clinically from primary thyroid disease except that TSH is low rather than high. Gonadotropin (LH/FSH) deficiency in women causes amenorrhea and oestrogen deficiency, in men causes hypogonadism and infertility. Growth hormone deficiency in adults produces an adverse metabolic phenotype with increased visceral fat, reduced muscle mass, fatigue, and impaired quality of life. ADH deficiency (cranial diabetes insipidus) causes massive urine output and extreme thirst requiring vasopressin replacement. Because multiple axes may be lost simultaneously or sequentially, ongoing surveillance for new deficiencies is a core part of long-term management.

typesClassification.description: Deficiency of a single pituitary hormone, most often isolated growth hormone deficiency in children or an isolated gonadotropin deficiency (Kallmann syndrome) in adults; other axes remain intact.
Deficiency of two or more pituitary hormones, typically from a pituitary adenoma, post-surgical damage, or irradiation, requiring replacement of each deficient axis.

Loss of all anterior pituitary hormones, sometimes with posterior pituitary involvement (diabetes insipidus); usually results from large tumours, severe head trauma, pituitary apoplexy, or extensive radiation.
Isolated or combined deficiency of antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin) from the posterior pituitary, causing enormous daily urine volumes of 3–20 litres and severe thirst.

Pituitary deficiency appearing months to years after traumatic brain injury, cranial surgery, or radiotherapy; often underdiagnosed because symptoms develop gradually and are attributed to the primary injury.

primarySymptoms: Profound fatigue and weakness that is disproportionate to activity, reflecting the combined metabolic impact of adrenal, thyroid, and growth hormone deficiencies.
Weight gain and redistribution of body fat to the trunk and abdomen, particularly from growth hormone deficiency, which shifts body composition toward increased visceral adiposity.
Cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, and slow thinking from secondary hypothyroidism when TSH secretion is impaired; these are identical to primary thyroid disease symptoms.
Nausea, vomiting, low blood pressure, and dizziness — symptoms of secondary adrenal insufficiency from ACTH deficiency, which become severe and life-threatening during illness or surgery.
Menstrual irregularity or complete loss of periods in women, caused by LH and FSH deficiency suppressing oestrogen production and ovulation.
Decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and reduced sperm count in men from gonadotropin-mediated testosterone deficiency.
Short stature and delayed puberty in children with growth hormone or gonadotropin deficiency, often the presenting concern in paediatric cases.
Severe thirst and large volumes of dilute urine — up to 10–20 litres a day — from cranial diabetes insipidus if the posterior pituitary is involved.
Headaches and visual field loss, typically bitemporal hemianopia, when a pituitary tumour is large enough to compress the optic chiasm.
Hypoglycaemia (low blood glucose), particularly in children, caused by ACTH and growth hormone deficiency reducing counterregulatory glucose responses.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance that has gradually worsened over months.
Loss of menstrual regularity or onset of secondary amenorrhea without an obvious explanation.
Slowly progressive headache localised to the front or around the eyes, particularly if new or changing in character.
Unexplained weight gain or a change in body composition with increased abdominal fat.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe headache with eye movement problems, visual loss, and altered consciousness — the presentation of pituitary apoplexy, a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate imaging.

Cardiovascular collapse, severe vomiting, extreme weakness, and confusion during a febrile illness, indicating an adrenal crisis from ACTH deficiency — requires immediate intramuscular or intravenous hydrocortisone.
Seizures or unconsciousness from severe hypoglycaemia in patients with combined ACTH and growth hormone deficiency.
Extreme dehydration, hypernatraemia (dangerously high blood sodium), and haemodynamic instability from uncontrolled diabetes insipidus.

causes.description: Pituitary adenomas are the most common cause of acquired hypopituitarism in adults; the tumour mass compresses and destroys normal anterior pituitary cells, while large tumours additionally affect the stalk and hypothalamus. Hormone deficiency correlates roughly with tumour size, with GH typically lost first, followed by LH/FSH, then TSH, and finally ACTH.

Transsphenoidal surgery for adenoma or other pituitary lesions can injure normal pituitary tissue; the risk of new post-operative hormone deficiency varies from less than 5% for microprolactinoma removal to over 80% after surgery for large craniopharyngiomas, depending on tumour size, invasiveness, and surgical technique.

causes.cause: Radiation therapy to the skull base or brain
causes.description: Irradiation of pituitary tumours, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, cranial malignancies, or prophylactic whole-brain radiation damages the hypothalamic-pituitary axis in a dose- and time-dependent manner; deficiencies can emerge years to decades after treatment, with GH loss earliest and most frequent.

Pituitary stalk damage from closed head injury disrupts blood supply and neural signalling to the anterior pituitary; studies find pituitary deficiency in 25–40% of patients with moderate-to-severe head injury, often missed during acute rehabilitation because fatigue and cognitive symptoms are attributed to the primary injury.

Sudden haemorrhage or ischaemic necrosis within a pre-existing adenoma destroys pituitary cells acutely; clinically it presents as a thunderclap headache with visual and ocular motor findings, and residual hypopituitarism develops in 80–90% of survivors if not surgically treated promptly.

Autoimmune lymphocytic hypophysitis, sarcoidosis, histiocytosis X (Langerhans cell histiocytosis), tuberculosis, and haemochromatosis can all infiltrate and destroy pituitary tissue; lymphocytic hypophysitis classically presents in women in late pregnancy or the postpartum period.

Severe obstetric haemorrhage causing ischaemic necrosis of the pituitary; the postpartum failure to lactate and absent menstrual recovery are the classic clues, though symptoms can be subtle and diagnosis delayed by years.

Craniopharyngioma, a tumour arising from Rathke's pouch remnants, frequently involves the hypothalamus and is the most common cause of hypopituitarism in children; complete resection usually causes panhypopituitarism, driving current practice toward subtotal removal plus radiotherapy to preserve hormone function where possible.

riskFactors.description: Any pituitary or hypothalamic tumour carries a risk of progressive hormone loss from direct compression or from its treatment; annual hormone surveillance is recommended.
Surgical disruption of the pituitary stalk or gland raises the risk of partial or complete hypopituitarism; risk increases with tumour size, invasiveness, and revision procedures.

Cumulative radiation doses above 40 Gy to the hypothalamic-pituitary axis substantially increase the lifetime risk of multiple hormone deficiencies; lower doses cause GH deficiency in a significant proportion of patients.

riskFactors.factor: Moderate to severe traumatic brain injury
riskFactors.description: Roughly 25–40% of TBI survivors develop at least one pituitary hormone deficiency; GH and gonadotropin deficiency are most common, often overlooked because symptoms blend with post-TBI neurological sequelae.
Women who experience severe postpartum haemorrhage (Sheehan syndrome) or who develop autoimmune lymphocytic hypophysitis during pregnancy are at specific risk of hypopituitarism.
Conditions such as sarcoidosis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis, and haemochromatosis can involve the pituitary; patients with these conditions warrant pituitary hormone testing if symptoms arise.

Cancer immunotherapy with CTLA-4 or PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors causes hypophysitis and hypopituitarism in 0.5–5% of patients; early recognition prevents adrenal crisis, and patients should be counselled to report fatigue, headache, or visual changes promptly.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 30–60 with pituitary adenomas, the most common adult cause
Children with craniopharyngioma, midline brain tumours, or congenital pituitary dysgenesis
Patients after traumatic brain injury, regardless of age
Women in the peripartum period, through Sheehan syndrome or lymphocytic hypophysitis
Cancer survivors who received cranial irradiation or checkpoint immunotherapy
Athletes and patients with a history of severe head injury, in whom diagnosis is often delayed
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of hypopituitarism requires demonstrating subnormal pituitary hormone levels or blunted responses to stimulation, interpreted in the context of the clinical picture and imaging findings. Basal hormone measurements provide the starting point: a morning serum cortisol below 100 nmol/L strongly suggests ACTH deficiency, while a level above 500 nmol/L makes it unlikely; the insulin tolerance test or short Synacthen test is used to confirm borderline results. Serum TSH with free T4 identifies secondary hypothyroidism — characteristically a low or inappropriately normal TSH with low free T4. Serum LH, FSH, and testosterone (men) or oestradiol with cycle history (women) assess the gonadal axis; low gonadotropins with low sex hormones confirm hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. Serum IGF-1 screens for GH deficiency, but definitive diagnosis requires a dynamic stimulation test such as the insulin tolerance test, glucagon stimulation, or GHRH-arginine test, because basal GH is uninterpretable as a single value. Serum prolactin and sodium, urine osmolality, and a water deprivation test are included when diabetes insipidus or stalk disruption is suspected. Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the pituitary is essential to identify the structural cause. Because deficiencies may emerge at different times after a pituitary insult, annual reassessment of all axes is standard practice in at-risk patients.

diagnosticTests.test: Morning serum cortisol and ACTH stimulation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses adrenal axis; a low morning cortisol or blunted cortisol response to synthetic ACTH (Synacthen) confirms secondary adrenal insufficiency — the most urgent deficiency to identify.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn before 9 a.m.; for stimulation testing, a small injection of synthetic ACTH is given and blood is drawn at 30 and 60 minutes after to measure the cortisol response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies secondary hypothyroidism; in hypopituitarism, free T4 is low while TSH is inappropriately low or normal, unlike primary hypothyroidism where TSH is high.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results returned within hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum gonadotropins (LH, FSH) and sex hormones
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypogonadotropic hypogonadism; low LH and FSH with low testosterone or oestradiol confirms gonadotropin deficiency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A morning blood draw; women are tested on day 2–5 of the cycle if menstruating, or at any time if amenorrheic.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum IGF-1 and GH stimulation test
diagnosticTests.purpose: IGF-1 below the age-adjusted reference range suggests GH deficiency; a glucagon stimulation or insulin tolerance test provides definitive dynamic assessment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: IGF-1 is a standard blood test. Stimulation tests take 2–3 hours and involve fasting, medication injection, and timed blood draws; close supervision is needed for the insulin tolerance test due to hypoglycaemia risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the structural cause — adenoma, craniopharyngioma, apoplexy, empty sella, or infiltrative disease — and guides whether surgery or radiotherapy is needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30–45 minute MRI scan with an intravenous contrast injection; thin-cut pituitary sequences are used.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum sodium, urine and plasma osmolality, water deprivation test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses cranial diabetes insipidus when posterior pituitary involvement is suspected; low urine osmolality despite high plasma osmolality and hypernatraemia is characteristic.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urine and blood are collected at baseline and after controlled water restriction under careful medical supervision; response to desmopressin injection confirms central versus nephrogenic cause.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects visual field loss from optic chiasm compression when a macroprolactinoma or other suprasellar mass is found on MRI.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20–30 minute ophthalmological test tracking peripheral light detection; repeated to monitor treatment response.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of hypopituitarism is primarily hormone replacement — substituting each deficient hormone to restore normal physiology and prevent the most dangerous consequences. The priority order reflects clinical urgency: adrenal insufficiency from ACTH deficiency is life-threatening and must be replaced first with hydrocortisone, typically 15–25 mg daily in two or three divided doses. Levothyroxine replaces thyroid hormone in secondary hypothyroidism; it must not be started before cortisol replacement is confirmed adequate, because thyroid hormone accelerates cortisol clearance and can precipitate an adrenal crisis in an untreated patient. Sex hormone replacement with testosterone in men or oestrogen-progesterone in women restores libido, bone density, and wellbeing. In adults with confirmed GH deficiency, recombinant human growth hormone given by nightly subcutaneous injection improves body composition, fatigue, bone density, and cardiovascular risk markers, though this treatment requires specialist prescription and monitoring. Cranial diabetes insipidus is treated with intranasal or oral desmopressin. The underlying cause also requires specific management: pituitary adenomas may need surgery, radiotherapy, or medical treatment; inflammatory causes respond to corticosteroids; craniopharyngiomas require surgery. Patients must carry a steroid emergency card and an injectable hydrocortisone kit, and must double or triple their hydrocortisone dose during intercurrent illness, surgery, or physiological stress — failure to do so is the most common cause of preventable death.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrocortisone (glucocorticoid replacement)
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of management for secondary adrenal insufficiency; given two or three times daily to mimic the normal diurnal cortisol pattern. Dose must be doubled or tripled during intercurrent illness, vomiting, or surgical stress. Patients carry injectable hydrocortisone for emergencies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reliably prevents adrenal crisis in over 95% of compliant patients; inadequate stress dosing is responsible for the majority of the estimated 6% annual crisis rate in adrenal insufficiency.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine (thyroid hormone replacement)
medicalTreatments.description: Replaces deficient thyroid hormone in secondary hypothyroidism; dosing is guided by free T4 levels in the mid-to-upper normal range rather than by TSH, which is unreliable as a monitoring target in pituitary disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises thyroid function and reverses symptoms of hypothyroidism in virtually all patients; dose adjustments are based on free T4 and clinical response.

medicalTreatments.name: Testosterone replacement (men)
medicalTreatments.description: Restores testosterone in men with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism via intramuscular injection, transdermal gel, or patch; improves libido, erection, bone density, muscle mass, and mood.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises serum testosterone in essentially all patients; bone density improvement of 2–8% over 2 years is well documented.

medicalTreatments.name: Oestrogen-progesterone replacement (women)
medicalTreatments.description: Hormonal replacement therapy restores oestrogen in premenopausal women with secondary hypogonadism, preventing bone loss and improving sexual function; progesterone is combined in women with an intact uterus. Fertility treatment requires gonadotropin injections, as endogenous gonadotropins cannot be replaced by their downstream hormones.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains bone density and relieves hypogonadal symptoms effectively; combined HRT reduces fracture risk by approximately 30–40% compared with untreated hypogonadism.

medicalTreatments.name: Recombinant human growth hormone (somatropin)
medicalTreatments.description: Daily subcutaneous injection restores GH in adults with confirmed GH deficiency; improves visceral fat distribution, muscle mass, bone density, exercise capacity, and quality of life scores.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces visceral fat mass by 10–20% and improves quality-of-life scores by clinically significant margins in approximately 75–80% of treated adults within 12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Desmopressin (for cranial diabetes insipidus)
medicalTreatments.description: A synthetic analogue of vasopressin given intranasally or orally to replace ADH; controls excessive urine output and thirst in posterior pituitary deficiency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces urine volume to near-normal levels in almost all patients with central diabetes insipidus when the dose is correctly titrated.

surgicalOptions.name: Transsphenoidal adenomectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic or microscopic removal of a pituitary adenoma causing hypopituitarism by mass effect; decompression of the remaining pituitary can sometimes allow partial hormone axis recovery, and visual field defects often improve.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant visual improvement in 70–90% of patients with chiasmal compression; full pituitary recovery occurs in a minority of cases, but stabilisation of further hormone loss is common.

surgicalOptions.name: Craniopharyngioma resection or debulking
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal or reduction of craniopharyngiomas causing hypopituitarism; complete resection carries very high rates of panhypopituitarism, so current practice favours subtotal resection followed by radiotherapy to balance tumour control with hormone preservation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Total gross resection achieves local control in about 80% of cases but causes panhypopituitarism in 80–90%; limited resection plus radiotherapy preserves more endocrine function with similar long-term disease control in many series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Lifestyle and physical activity
alternativeTreatments.description: Supervised exercise programmes, particularly resistance training combined with aerobic activity, can partially offset the metabolic effects of GH deficiency (increased visceral fat, reduced muscle mass) in patients who decline or cannot access GH replacement, though they do not substitute for hormone replacement.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological support and cognitive rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Growth hormone deficiency and chronic hypoadrenalism are independently associated with impaired quality of life, cognitive difficulties, and depression; cognitive behavioural therapy and structured support programmes are helpful adjuncts to hormone replacement.

whySeeSpecialist: Hypopituitarism requires an endocrinologist for initial diagnosis, hormone axis testing, and prescription of specialist treatments including recombinant GH and intranasal desmopressin. Neurosurgery and radiation oncology are needed when a structural cause requires intervention. Ophthalmology monitors visual fields in patients with suprasellar disease. Long-term endocrine follow-up is essential because new deficiencies can emerge years after a pituitary insult.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an endocrinologist with specific experience in pituitary disease, ideally within a multidisciplinary pituitary unit that includes neurosurgery, neuroradiology, and ophthalmology. Ask about their approach to GH replacement assessment, adrenal crisis prevention education, and how they monitor patients for late-appearing deficiencies after irradiation or surgery.
preventionStrategies: Ensure patients receiving cranial irradiation are enrolled in post-treatment endocrine surveillance protocols to detect hormone deficiencies early.
Screen all patients with moderate-to-severe traumatic brain injury for pituitary deficiency at 3–6 months and 12 months after injury.
Educate patients on immunotherapy about early symptoms of hypophysitis and hypopituitarism so that adrenal crises are prevented.
Provide all patients with secondary adrenal insufficiency with a steroid emergency card, an injectable hydrocortisone kit, and clear sick-day rules.
Monitor pituitary function annually in patients with known pituitary tumours or after pituitary surgery, as deficiencies can evolve over time.
lifestyleModifications: Never miss hydrocortisone doses; take the morning dose immediately on waking and do not delay it.
Double or triple hydrocortisone during fever, vomiting, injury, or surgical procedures and seek medical review if unsure.
Carry a steroid emergency injection kit and ensure close contacts know how to administer it.
Wear a medical alert bracelet or carry a wallet card stating the diagnosis and the need for emergency steroids.
Maintain regular moderate exercise to support bone density and cardiovascular health while on hormone replacement.
Attend all scheduled endocrine follow-up appointments, as dose adjustments and monitoring of bone density, metabolic markers, and tumour surveillance are ongoing.

recommended: Adequate calcium (1,000–1,500 mg/day) and vitamin D to protect bone density, particularly when oestrogen or testosterone replacement is delayed
A balanced diet to support cardiovascular health, which is impaired in GH-deficient patients
Regular protein intake to maintain muscle mass in the context of GH and androgen deficiency
Staying well hydrated and monitoring fluid intake carefully in patients on desmopressin for diabetes insipidus
avoid: High sodium intake in patients on desmopressin, which can cause dangerous water retention and low sodium
Excess alcohol, which suppresses pituitary function and impairs bone density
Crash diets or severe caloric restriction, which can worsen the metabolic effects of GH deficiency

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise — brisk walking, swimming, cycling, and resistance training — is recommended for all patients with hypopituitarism to offset the adverse body composition effects of GH deficiency and hypogonadism. Patients must ensure they have taken their hydrocortisone before exercising, and during intense or prolonged exercise, an additional supplementary hydrocortisone dose (5–10 mg) may be needed to prevent adrenal insufficiency symptoms. High-intensity exercise in extreme heat, which markedly increases cortisol requirements, warrants extra caution.
dailyManagement: Take hydrocortisone at fixed times each day; the largest dose on waking and a smaller afternoon dose.
Carry emergency injectable hydrocortisone and ensure family members or close contacts are trained to use it.
Take levothyroxine on an empty stomach, at least 30 minutes before food, for consistent absorption.
Maintain a symptom diary — tracking fatigue, weight, mood, and headaches helps identify inadequate replacement or emerging new deficiencies.
Attend pituitary MRI surveillance at the interval recommended by the pituitary team to monitor tumour status.
prognosis: With complete and well-managed hormone replacement, most patients with hypopituitarism live near-normal lives and have excellent symptom control. However, several population studies show that overall mortality in hypopituitarism is modestly elevated compared with age-matched controls, largely driven by cardiovascular disease and adrenal crisis in those with ACTH deficiency. Growth hormone replacement in adults with confirmed GH deficiency improves body composition, bone density, and quality of life, and may reduce cardiovascular risk. The prognosis for fertility depends on the degree of gonadotropin deficiency; exogenous gonadotropin therapy (gonadotropins injected to stimulate follicle development or spermatogenesis) achieves pregnancy in many couples. Visual defects from optic chiasm compression often improve substantially after pituitary tumour decompression. Long-term prognosis is most closely tied to the underlying cause and whether it can be controlled.
recoveryTimeline: Adrenal and thyroid replacement begin providing symptom relief within days to weeks of starting treatment. Testosterone or oestrogen replacement improves libido and mood within 4–8 weeks and bone density over 12–24 months. Growth hormone treatment produces perceptible changes in body composition — reduced abdominal fat and improved energy — within 3–6 months, with further gains over 1–2 years. Visual field defects from chiasmal compression typically begin improving within weeks of surgical decompression or initiation of dopamine agonist therapy for a prolactinoma. New hormone deficiencies from radiation can emerge over years, so ongoing surveillance rather than a defined end-point defines the recovery process.
complications: Adrenal crisis — sudden, life-threatening cortisol deficiency precipitated by illness, surgery, or vomiting; presents with hypotension, severe vomiting, altered consciousness, and hypoglycaemia requiring emergency intramuscular hydrocortisone.

Cardiovascular disease — GH deficiency and hypogonadism both increase visceral fat, insulin resistance, and adverse lipid profiles, contributing to a two- to three-fold higher cardiovascular event rate in some cohort studies.

Osteoporosis and fracture — untreated or inadequately replaced sex hormone deficiency causes progressive bone mineral density loss; vertebral and hip fractures occur at rates higher than the general population.

Infertility — hypogonadotropic hypogonadism prevents normal ovulation and spermatogenesis; although treatable with gonadotropin therapy in motivated couples, this requires specialist input and is not always successful.
Visual field loss — unrecognised or untreated pituitary mass effect causes progressive bitemporal vision loss that may become permanent if optic chiasm compression is prolonged.

Cranial diabetes insipidus complications — uncontrolled or untreated posterior pituitary involvement causes extreme dehydration, dangerous hypernatraemia, and electrolyte disturbances that are potentially fatal.

supportResources.name: Pituitary Foundation — Hypopituitarism Information
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient guidance on understanding, managing, and living with hypopituitarism from the UK's leading pituitary patient charity.

supportResources.name: The Pituitary Network Association
supportResources.description: US-based organisation providing peer support, specialist directories, and educational resources for patients with pituitary and hypothalamic disorders.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Hypopituitarism
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases covering causes, diagnosis, and treatment.

sources.title: Melmed S et al. Pituitary-Tumor Endocrinopathies. New England Journal of Medicine, 2020
Fleseriu M et al. Hormonal Replacement in Hypopituitarism in Adults: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline, 2016
Schneider HJ et al. Hypothalamopituitary Dysfunction following Traumatic Brain Injury and Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Haemorrhage. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism, 2008
Bates AS et al. The Effect of Hypopituitarism on Life Expectancy. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism, 1996
Tomlinson JW et al. Association between Premature Mortality and Hypopituitarism. Lancet, 2001
Webb SM, Strauss K, Gadelha MR et al. International Multicenter Study of Occurrence and Management of Hypopituitarism. European Journal of Endocrinology, 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain, but in primary hypothyroidism the TSH is elevated, while in secondary (pituitary) hypothyroidism from hypopituitarism the TSH is inappropriately low or normal; free T4 is low in both.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cushing syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cushing syndrome involves cortisol excess causing weight gain, hypertension, and stretch marks; hypopituitarism involves cortisol deficiency causing weight loss, low blood pressure, and fatigue — opposite physiological states that can both originate from a pituitary tumour.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetes insipidus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Central diabetes insipidus is a component of hypopituitarism when the posterior pituitary is affected, but it can occur in isolation from various causes without any anterior pituitary deficiency; distinguishing complete from partial hypopituitarism requires formal hormone testing beyond the water deprivation test.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia and chronic fatigue
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The fatigue, pallor, and weakness of hypopituitarism can closely mimic iron-deficiency or chronic disease anaemia; the distinguishing feature is the combination of low sex hormone levels, weight change, and abnormal pituitary hormone profile rather than a haematological defect alone.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperprolactinemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Large prolactinomas are a major cause of hypopituitarism through compression; elevated prolactin and pituitary hormone deficiency frequently coexist in macroprolactinoma patients.

relatedConditions.name: Acromegaly
relatedConditions.relevance: GH-secreting adenomas can compress normal pituitary tissue and cause co-existing hypopituitarism; after treatment, residual or new deficiencies must be assessed.

relatedConditions.name: Cushing syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Pituitary-dependent Cushing disease (ACTH-secreting adenoma) involves the same gland; treatment of Cushing disease with surgery or pituitary irradiation commonly produces hypopituitarism requiring replacement.

relatedConditions.name: Drug-induced hypopituitarism
relatedConditions.relevance: Checkpoint immunotherapy-induced hypophysitis is an increasingly common cause of hypopituitarism in cancer patients; recognition and corticosteroid management are needed before hormone replacement.

faqs.question: What is hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Hypopituitarism means the pituitary gland at the base of the brain is not producing enough of one or more hormones that control the thyroid, adrenal glands, reproductive organs, growth, and water balance. It can affect a single hormone or all of them.

faqs.question: What causes hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are a pituitary tumour (adenoma), surgery or radiotherapy to the pituitary area, head injury, and pituitary haemorrhage (apoplexy). Rarer causes include autoimmune inflammation, infiltrative diseases, and severe obstetric haemorrhage (Sheehan syndrome).

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Symptoms depend on which hormones are missing. Common ones include severe fatigue, low blood pressure, weight gain or loss, irregular periods or loss of libido, feeling cold, and — if the pituitary tumour is large — headaches and vision problems. Low cortisol is the most dangerous deficiency.

faqs.question: Is hypopituitarism life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Untreated ACTH deficiency (secondary adrenal insufficiency) can cause a life-threatening adrenal crisis during illness or surgery. With correct hormone replacement and a sick-day action plan, the condition is well controlled and rarely fatal, though all patients should carry emergency injectable hydrocortisone.

faqs.question: How is hypopituitarism treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment replaces each deficient hormone: hydrocortisone for adrenal insufficiency, levothyroxine for thyroid deficiency, testosterone or oestrogen for sex hormone deficiency, growth hormone injections for GH deficiency, and desmopressin for diabetes insipidus. The underlying cause may also need surgery or radiotherapy.

faqs.question: Can hypopituitarism be cured?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. Removing a pituitary tumour sometimes allows partial hormone recovery, but most patients need lifelong replacement of at least some hormones. Cases caused by correctable inflammation or medication may fully resolve.

faqs.question: What is panhypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Panhypopituitarism means all or nearly all anterior pituitary hormones are deficient. It usually results from large tumours, pituitary apoplexy, or extensive surgery or radiotherapy. Every deficient hormone axis must be replaced with the appropriate medication.

faqs.question: What is a steroid emergency card?
faqs.answer: A steroid emergency card notifies medical staff that you have adrenal insufficiency and need emergency hydrocortisone if you are unconscious, vomiting, or having surgery. Patients with ACTH deficiency must carry one at all times, as this information can prevent a preventable death.

faqs.question: Can I still have children with hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Many people with gonadotropin deficiency can conceive with specialist fertility treatment using injected gonadotropins to stimulate egg development or sperm production. This treatment takes several months and requires a fertility specialist working with your endocrinologist.

faqs.question: Does hypopituitarism cause weight gain?
faqs.answer: Growth hormone deficiency causes increased visceral fat and reduced muscle mass, which can appear as weight gain and a thickened waist. Low thyroid and sex hormones also affect metabolism. GH replacement and sex hormone treatment help correct these changes over 6–12 months.

faqs.question: How is growth hormone deficiency diagnosed in adults?
faqs.answer: A low blood IGF-1 level is a useful screen, but definitive diagnosis requires a stimulation test such as the glucagon stimulation or insulin tolerance test, which measures peak GH release. A single random GH level cannot diagnose deficiency and should not be used.

faqs.question: What is sick-day rule management?
faqs.answer: Sick-day rules instruct patients with adrenal insufficiency to double or triple their hydrocortisone dose whenever they have a fever above 38°C, vomiting, or undergo surgery or injury. If they cannot take oral medication, they must inject hydrocortisone immediately and seek hospital care.

faqs.question: Can head injury cause hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to 25–40% of people with moderate-to-severe traumatic brain injury develop pituitary hormone deficiency, most commonly growth hormone and gonadotropin deficiency. Symptoms often overlap with post-concussion fatigue and cognitive problems, so testing is needed to distinguish them.

faqs.question: How often should I have hormone levels tested with hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Typically every 6–12 months when on stable replacement therapy, plus additional checks after any change in dose, illness, or surgery. Pituitary MRI is usually repeated annually or every 1–2 years depending on the underlying cause and tumour status.

faqs.question: Does hypopituitarism cause depression?
faqs.answer: Low cortisol, thyroid hormone, sex hormones, and growth hormone are all independently associated with depression, fatigue, and reduced motivation. Adequate hormone replacement significantly improves mood in most patients, though psychological support may also be needed.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hypopituitarism and hypothyroidism?
faqs.answer: In primary hypothyroidism the thyroid gland itself fails and TSH is high. In hypopituitarism the pituitary fails to produce adequate TSH so the thyroid is under-stimulated — TSH is low or normal with a low free T4. The hormone that needs replacing is the same (levothyroxine) but monitoring uses free T4 rather than TSH.

faqs.question: Can cancer immunotherapy cause hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Yes. Checkpoint inhibitor drugs (PD-1 and CTLA-4 inhibitors) used to treat cancer cause immune-mediated inflammation of the pituitary (hypophysitis) in 0.5–5% of patients, leading to hypopituitarism. Patients should report fatigue, headache, or visual changes promptly during immunotherapy.

faqs.question: What is Sheehan syndrome?
faqs.answer: Sheehan syndrome is hypopituitarism caused by severe blood loss during or after childbirth, which deprives the enlarged postpartum pituitary of blood and causes infarction. Failure to produce breast milk and absent return of periods are the classic first signs. It is rare in countries with modern obstetric care.

faqs.question: Is growth hormone replacement safe in adults?
faqs.answer: Recombinant growth hormone is generally safe at the low doses used for adult GH deficiency. Side effects — fluid retention, joint pains, and carpal tunnel — are usually dose-related and settle with dose reduction. Long-term cardiovascular safety appears favourable. Treatment is not recommended without confirmed GH deficiency.

faqs.question: What does the pituitary gland control?
faqs.answer: The pituitary controls the thyroid (via TSH), adrenal glands (via ACTH), reproductive system (via LH and FSH), body growth (via GH), breast milk production (via prolactin), and water balance (via ADH from the posterior pituitary). Damage to the gland can therefore affect multiple organ systems simultaneously.

metaDescription: Hypopituitarism is pituitary hormone deficiency causing fatigue, adrenal crisis risk, and infertility. Learn causes, diagnosis and hormone replacement therapy.
